headline: Bobby Witt Jr. Goes First Overall, Benchwarmers Get Robbed by Snake Math, and the 2026 BeerLeague Title Is Already Decided
subheadline: Your definitive, completely unhinged breakdown of every pick from the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft — steals, disasters, bold predictions, and the one team you should already be scared of.
body:
## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *Goodfellas* where Henry Hill is narrating everything happening at once — the helicopter, the drugs, the paranoia — and you realize he's been making decisions on pure instinct and adrenaline the whole time? That's the BeerLeagueBaseball draft in a nutshell. Fourteen managers, twelve categories, one snake draft, and roughly 210 picks standing between them and either a championship or another year of texting into the void about how their team "had bad luck with injuries." (Spoiler: it wasn't bad luck. It was the draft.) The 2026 edition went down on March 22nd, and I was locked in from the jump — coffee in hand, spreadsheet open, already judging everyone.

Here's what we already know walking out of the war room: **North Correa** finally stopped the bleeding and grabbed the best player on the board at Pick 1. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** are quietly terrifying, like a *No Country for Old Men* Anton Chigurh situation where you don't realize how good they are until it's too late. **General Hospital** has now finished 13th and 13th in back-to-back years and somehow still managed to draft like a team that thinks finishing 13th is fine, actually. And the **Benchwarmers** — the team picking last — somehow turned the most cursed draft slot into the most interesting roster in the league. The snake giveth, and the snake taketh away.

The big storylines: Was **Bobby Witt Jr.** the right call at 1.01, or should **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** have gone first? Was the **Canberra Wombats'** second-round pivot to **Yordan Alvarez** the steal of the draft? And can someone — *anyone* — explain to me what **Athletic Park Dragons** were doing drafting pitchers in rounds 4 through 8 after finishing dead last in 2025? Pull up a chair. This is going to take a while.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The top of the 2026 draft was genuinely stacked — like, *Entourage* pilot episode where every name on the call sheet is interesting stacked. **Bobby Witt Jr.** going first overall to **North Correa** at Pick 1 is completely defensible. The guy hit .332 with 33 home runs, 109 RBI, and 31 stolen bases in 2025, playing one of the most valuable positions in fantasy. He's the rare five-category monster who doesn't require you to apologize to anyone. **Athletic Park Dragons** grabbing **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at Pick 2 is also locked-in correct — even in his batter-only slot, Ohtani's 2025 was otherworldly (35 HR, 96 RBI, 40 SB, .390 OBP — yes, the man stole 40 bases). The Ohtani vs. Witt debate is real and I respect both sides.

What I can't fully respect is **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** taking **Mookie Betts** at Pick 3. Look, Mookie is Mookie — elite hit tool, five positions of eligibility, .307 average, 24 HR in 2025. But the injury cloud hanging over Betts is real, and taking him ahead of **Aaron Judge** (Pick 4, 58 HR and .975 OPS in 2025) feels like choosing the Audi over the Ferrari because the Audi has better cup holders. Judge at Pick 4 to **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** is the best pure value in the top five. **Elly De La Cruz** at 5 to **BaseballPro77** (37 HR, 67 SB, absolutely absurd) and **José Ramírez** at 6 to **Albuquerque Bombers** (39 HR, 118 RBI, eight straight All-Star seasons) round out a first round with essentially zero genuine disasters — which, honestly, is boring. The chaos we needed didn't arrive until Round 2.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**1. North Correa** | Draft Slot #1

Coming off back-to-back finishes of 14th and 12th, **North Correa** needed a reset, and grabbing **Bobby Witt Jr.** first overall is exactly the kind of franchise-cornerstone move that changes culture. The follow-through is where it gets dicey. **Pete Alonso** in Round 2 (Pick 28) is solid — 40 HR, 1B eligibility — but the pitching staff assembled here reads like a trauma ward. **Sandy Alcantara** (Round 8) is coming off Tommy John. **Spencer Strider** (Round 9) is coming off Tommy John. **Brandon Woodruff** (Round 14) is coming off... you don't want to know. This is a rotation built entirely on rehab timelines and prayer. **Logan Gilbert** in Round 3 is the one dependable arm here (3.37 ERA, 208 K in 2025). The outfield depth with **Michael Harris II**, **Lawrence Butler**, and **Dylan Crews** is intriguing but unproven. A foundation is being laid, but we've heard that about North Correa before.

**Grade: B−** | Witt is a cheat code, but the pitching gambles could detonate this roster like a faulty microwave.

---

**2. Athletic Park Dragons** | Draft Slot #2

The Dragons finished 14th in 2025 — dead last — after a 6th-place finish in 2024. **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at Pick 2 is obviously correct. Everything after that is... an identity crisis. **Matt Olson** (Round 2) hit 38 HR with a .365 OBP in 2025 — good pick. But then Rounds 4 through 8 are entirely starting pitchers: **Michael King**, **Shota Imanaga**, **Aaron Nola**, **Luis Castillo**, and **Andrés Muñoz** (who's a reliever, fine). There's a philosophy here — build around Ohtani's bat and construct a pitching fortress — but the hitting depth is borderline catastrophic. **Kyle Schwarber** (Round 3) gives you power and absolutely zero average. **Ezequiel Tovar** (Round 10) hits in Colorado, which is the draft equivalent of ordering a salad from a steakhouse.

**Grade: C+** | Great foundation, suspiciously thin middle, and the late rounds look like they drafted with autocomplete.

---

**3. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Draft Slot #3

**Mookie Betts** at 3 is the pick I'd argue with at a bar for two hours. But let's give **Wisconsin Rapids** their flowers for Round 2 — **Ketel Marte** (Pick 26) is an underrated stud, .290/.355/.470 in 2025, and the 2B eligibility is gold. **William Contreras** (Round 3) is a top-five catcher. The problem is this team has basically zero power — Mookie, Marte, **Bryan Reynolds**, **Christian Yelich** — it's a lot of "good average" bats that don't move the needle in the HR and RBI categories. **Isaac Paredes** (Round 7) helps with the pop, but he's a 3B/1B on Houston, which feels like a weird fit. The bullpen (Justin Martinez, Jeff Hoffman, Jason Foley, Jason Adam) is all dice rolls. The Reapers finished 10th in 2025 in their first year, and this draft has the feel of a team still figuring out what they want to be.

**Grade: C+** | Lovely pieces, no real identity, and not enough thunder bats to win a power-heavy league.

---

**4. Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Draft Slot #4

The defending champion (2024) and perennial contender — three straight top-10 finishes, one title. **Aaron Judge** at Pick 4 is the easy button in the best possible way. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2 gives them a second SB source with three-position eligibility. **Austin Riley** (Round 3) is a 35 HR, .800+ OPS stalwart. The rotation — **Pablo López**, **Spencer Schwellenbach**, **Zac Gallen**, **Kevin Gausman** — is deep and reliable, even if none of them are aces. **Brice Turang** (Round 8) is a nice SB stash. **Xavier Edwards** (Round 9) could contribute a sneaky .300+ average. This team does what champions do: they don't panic, they don't reach, and they build depth in the middle rounds without sacrificing the top.

**Grade: A−** | Methodical, deep, and built like a team that's been here before — because they have.

---

**5. BaseballPro77** | Draft Slot #5

Two-time champion (2023, 2025), perennial contender, absolutely maddening to play against. **Elly De La Cruz** at Pick 5 is electric — the man is a one-man stolen base factory with 30+ HR upside. **Jackson Merrill** (Round 2) had a monster 2025 breakout, 24 HR with 15 SB. **Cole Ragans** (Round 3) is a legitimate SP1 candidate (3.18 ERA, 217 K in 2025). **Emmanuel Clase** (Round 4) is the best closer in baseball. The back end is where this gets interesting — **Hunter Greene** (Round 9) is a strikeout machine if the ERA cooperates, and **Royce Lewis** (Round 12) is a massive upside stash. **Bo Bichette** (Round 11) going to the Mets adds lineup context. This is a scary roster from a scary manager who has already won twice.

**Grade: A** | The team most likely to make everyone else feel like they drafted wrong.

---

**6. Albuquerque Bombers** | Draft Slot #6

Coming off a 3rd-place finish in 2025, the Bombers are knocking on the door. **José Ramírez** at Pick 6 is a perennial monster — 39 HR, 118 RBI, 26 SB, .872 OPS. **Freddie Freeman** (Round 2) gives them an elite 1B to pair. **Garrett Crochet** (Round 3) was arguably a steal — the Red Sox lefty had a 3.22 ERA and 226 K in 2025 and is an SP1. **Logan Webb** (Round 5) is consistent and productive. **Willy Adames** (Round 6) adds middle infield depth. The concern is the back half — **Colton Cowser**, **Jasson Domínguez**, and **Tyler O'Neill** are all high-variance roll-the-dice picks. But the foundation here is legitimately terrifying.

**Grade: A−** | This team should be third on your "teams I'm afraid of" list and I wouldn't blame you for putting them first.

---

**7. Hiroshima Radiation** | Draft Slot #7

The Radiation's finish history reads like a heartbeat monitor — 2nd, 11th, 5th — and this draft feels similarly unpredictable. **Juan Soto** at Pick 7 is elite (41 HR, .419 OBP, 109 RBI with the Mets in 2025). **Trea Turner** (Round 2) gives them speed at SS. **Blake Snell** (Round 3) is the ultimate risk-reward arm. **Tyler Glasnow** (Round 5) is another massive injury gamble. There's a theme here: this manager is swinging for the fences in every category, accepting variance as the cost of doing business. It might work brilliantly. It might also fall apart by June when Glasnow pulls a hamstring getting out of his car.

**Grade: B** | Electric ceiling, volatile floor, certified chaos agent.

---

**8. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Draft Slot #8

Back-to-back top-five finishes (5th, 2nd), the Wonderdogs might be the scariest team in this draft. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at Pick 8 is a steal at his slot — 34 HR, .930 OPS, 1B/3B eligibility. **Bryce Harper** (Round 2) gives them a second elite 1B. **Corbin Burnes** (Round 3) is a horse (3.01 ERA, 231 K in 2025). **Framber Valdez** (Round 4) is the ultimate QS machine. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** (Round 5) is coming back from his ACL and — if healthy — is the steal of this entire draft at Pick 64. **Devin Williams** (Round 7) is an elite closer. **George Kirby** (Round 10) and **Seth Lugo** (Round 11) provide unreal rotation depth. This roster is DEEP.

**Grade: A+** | The team nobody is talking about enough — and the one most likely to be hoisting at the end.

---

**9. Buttville Skidmarks** | Draft Slot #9

The Skidmarks have been frustratingly average — 11th, 8th, 8th — and this draft feels like a make-or-break moment. **Kyle Tucker** at Pick 9 is an elite OF (29 HR, 22 SB, .878 OPS joining the Dodgers). **Zack Wheeler** (Round 2) is a workhorse ace. **Rafael Devers** (Round 3) gives them a 30 HR 3B. **Corey Seager** (Round 4) is a premium SS on a good Texas lineup. The concern: **Jacob deGrom** (Round 5, Texas Rangers) is a massive health gamble — genius-level upside, "maybe he pitches 60 innings" risk. The roster balance is actually strong, and this feels like the team most likely to break through into the top five.

**Grade: B+** | The Skidmarks have all the ingredients; they just need deGrom not to explode.

---

**10. General Hospital** | Draft Slot #10

Finished 13th in 2024. Finished 13th in 2025. At some point this becomes a bit. **Francisco Lindor** at Pick 10 is fine — 31 HR, 98 RBI, elite defense that doesn't help you in fantasy, but the bat is real. **Jarren Duran** (Round 2) is a legit .290/25 HR/30 SB guy. **Chris Sale** (Round 3, Atlanta) is fascinating — when he's on, he's an SP1. **Mike Trout** (Round 6) at that ADP is either the greatest steal of the draft or the most expensive jar of empty promises in baseball. **Max Fried** (Round 5, Yankees) is legitimately good. This team has the pieces to finally escape 13th. But they've said that before.

**Grade: B−** | Love the optimism, worried about the execution, cannot shake the haunting feeling that they'll finish 13th again.

---

**11. Sugar Land Skeeters** | Draft Slot #11

Reigning champion (2025), and the Skeeters are not messing around. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at Pick 11 (35 HR, 25 SB, .880 OPS in 2025) is legitimate. **Tarik Skubal** (Round 2) is arguably the best pitcher taken in this draft — 2.45 ERA, 228 K, 2025 Cy Young favorite. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** (Round 4) anchors a rotation that also features **Ryan Helsley** (elite closer). **Cody Bellinger** (Round 3) is a risky pick after his 2025 regression but has upside in Yankee Stadium. This is a defending champion playing with house money and drafting smart.

**Grade: A−** | Defending champs, ace pitcher, elite closer. See you in the finals.

---

**12. Riverside Roughnecks** | Draft Slot #12

Three straight finishes in the 9-11 range — good enough to not be embarrassed, not good enough to actually do anything. **Jackson Chourio** at Pick 12 is a potential steal — the 21-year-old Brewers OF hit 24 HR with 23 SB in 2025 and is trending up. **Gunnar Henderson** (Round 2) is a top-20 pick value at that slot. **Mason Miller** (Round 6) is an elite closer. **Roki Sasaki** (Round 7) is a fascinating flier on a pitcher with ace stuff learning MLB. **James Wood** (Round 5) could be the best late-bloomer pick in the draft. The Roughnecks are quietly assembling a team that could surprise everyone.

**Grade: B+** | Finally building something real. Don't sleep on them.

---

**13. Canberra Wombats** | Draft Slot #13

Finished 4th in 2025, and the Wombats have the feel of a team that's always close. **Corbin Carroll** at Pick 13 is the kind of speed/average/SB profile that wins categories. **Yordan Alvarez** (Round 2, Pick 16) is the pick of the draft — the man missed significant time in 2025 and still mashed, and getting him at 16 is borderline criminal value. **Wyatt Langford** (Round 3) is a rising star. **Josh Hader** (Round 5) is the elite closer you need. **Cal Raleigh** (Round 6) is a top-3 catcher. **Kodai Senga** (Round 12) and **Shane McClanahan** (Round 13) are high-upside late-round fliers. This roster is balanced, deep, and infuriating to play against.

**Grade: A−** | Alvarez alone makes this an A-grade draft.

---

**14. Benchwarmers** | Draft Slot #14

Dead last in 2023, 7th in 2024, 9th in 2025 — the Benchwarmers have the most interesting trajectory in the league. And drafting from Pick 14, they turned lemons into absolute lemonade. **Julio Rodríguez** (Pick 14) plus **Paul Skenes** (Round 2, Pick 15) is a back-to-back bonanza that no one else in this draft got to experience. Skenes was the best pitcher in baseball over the second half of 2025 (1.98 ERA over his last 15 starts), and getting him at 15 is value that makes me want to audit the ADP calculations. **Oneil Cruz** (Round 3), **Dylan Cease** (Round 4), **Edwin Díaz** (Round 5) — this roster goes deep and stays interesting.

**Grade: A** | The snake draft's greatest beneficiary, and now a legitimate title threat.

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The Three Best Value Picks:**

1. **Yordan Alvarez, Canberra Wombats, Round 2, Pick 16** — ADP had Alvarez closer to Pick 10-12 range. Getting a fully healthy 40 HR masher with a .420 OBP ceiling this late in Round 2 is highway robbery. If the Wombats win the title, this is where they won it.

2. **Paul Skenes, Benchwarmers, Round 2, Pick 15** — A pitcher who posted a sub-2.00 ERA in the second half of 2025 falling to Pick 15 is the kind of draft result that makes people flip tables. Benchwarmers got a generational arm at a discount, and the snake turn gave them both J-Rod AND Skenes in consecutive picks. Obscene.

3. **Garrett Crochet, Albuquerque Bombers, Round 3, Pick 34** — The Red Sox lefty was arguably a top-20 pitcher going into 2026 drafts, and Bombers got him in the third round. 226 strikeouts, 3.22 ERA, and elite WHIP. This is the kind of pick that wins championships.

**The Three Worst Reaches:**

1. **Ezequiel Tovar, Athletic Park Dragons, Round 10, Pick 139** — Tovar plays for the Rockies. His counting stats are inflated by Coors and his road numbers are quietly ugly. At Round 10 it's fine, but drafting a guy whose "value" lives and dies with thin air at altitude is always dicey.

2. **Nolan Arenado, Buttville Skidmarks, Round 15, Pick 205** — The Arenado who showed up to Arizona in 2025 is not the Arenado we remember. He's a declining bat with great glove metrics that don't register in fantasy. Round 15 isn't a disaster, but you can find better upside.

3. **Kenley Jansen, Athletic Park Dragons, Round 15, Pick 198** — Jansen is 38 years old. Drafting him as your final pick either means you're genuinely desperate for saves or you're feeling nostalgic for 2018. Neither is a great place to be.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **The Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs win the championship.** Ronald Acuña Jr. comes back healthy in May, erupts for 28 HR and 40 SB in the second half, and vaults the Wonderdogs from dark horse to title winner. Corbin Burnes goes 18-6 with a 2.85 ERA. Nobody sees it coming. Everyone acts like they saw it coming after.

2. **General Hospital finishes 13th for the third consecutive year.** I know. I KNOW. But Mike Trout goes on the IL in June, Chris Sale pitches 80 innings before his arm falls off, and the team finishes one spot ahead of whoever drafts the worst. This is their destiny now.

3. **Paul Skenes is the best pitcher in BeerLeagueBaseball by August.** Benchwarmers' Round 2 pick becomes the most talked-about value pick in league history when Skenes puts up a 2.10 ERA with 240 strikeouts over a full season. Everyone who passed on him will claim they had him rated higher.

4. **BaseballPro77 becomes the first team to win three championships.** The two-time champ (2023, 2025) has Elly De La Cruz, Emmanuel Clase, Cole Ragans, and Jackson Merrill. This lineup scores in every category. You've been warned.

5. **Canberra Wombats' Yordan Alvarez finishes as the #2 overall fantasy player.** Everyone was scared off by 2025 injury concerns. A fully healthy Alvarez hitting cleanup in Houston goes 45 HR, 120 RBI, .300 average, .420 OBP. Wombats ride him to a top-three finish.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Here's my call, and I'm making it with conviction: **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** are winning the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball championship.

Look at this roster. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** is a first-round talent at a discount. **Bryce Harper** gives you elite 1B production and an .850+ OPS. **Corbin Burnes** and **Framber Valdez** are the best pitching 1-2 punch in this draft — not close. **Devin Williams** is a lockdown closer. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at Round 5, Pick 64 is the ultimate lottery ticket from a guy who — when healthy — is the most exciting player in baseball. And then look at the depth: **George Kirby**, **Seth Lugo**, **Tanner Houck**, **Riley Greene**. This team doesn't have one weak column in the 12-category lineup.

They've finished 4th, 5th, and 2nd in three years. They have the roster construction of a champion. They drafted like a team that's been preparing for this moment, not reacting to it. In a league full of chaos agents and boom-or-bust rosters, the Wonderdogs are the team that makes sense — and in fantasy baseball, the team that makes sense usually makes you regret everything when October arrives.

The Wonderdogs are coming. And just like Lester Freamon in *The Wire*, they've been laying pipe all draft long — you just didn't notice until the bodies started dropping.

*See you in the championship round.*
